name: Ruta del Tambor y el Bombo Cycling Loop
name_original: Desierto de Calanda por el Río Martín - ida y vuelta desde Híjar
description: Cycle 33.2 miles (53.5 km) through the semi-desert landscapes of Desierto de Calanda and the Río Martín valley from Híjar.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Samper de Calanda traverse a landscape characterized by the fertile Martín River Valley and the Ebro River, offering diverse terrain for cyclists. The region features a blend of agricultural areas, natural settings, and historical landmarks. Cyclists can explore dedicated paths like the "Vía Verde," a greenway repurposed from an old railway line, or navigate "lonely roads" in the broader Matarraña and Bajo Aragón areas. The topography includes gentle river plains and ascents, providing varied road cycling terrain around Samper de Calanda. Best road cycling routes around Samper de Calanda The most popular road cycling route is Ruta del Tambor y el Bombo Cycling Loop, a 33.2 miles (53.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 23 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores a culturally significant area. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ariño – Andorra loop from Urrea de Gaén, a difficult 45.8 miles (73.8 km) path. This route connects towns through varied terrain, including significant elevation changes. Local road cyclists also love the Oliete – Ariño loop from La Puebla de Híjar, a 69.8 miles (112.3 km) trail leading through the wider region, often completed in about 5 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Samper de Calanda is defined by the River Martín Valley, the Ebro River, and the "Vía Verde" greenway. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging long-distance routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 170 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Samper de Calanda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Samper de Calanda on komoot?
A: Komoot features over 15 road cycling routes in the Samper de Calanda area. These routes cater to various preferences, including easy, moderate, and difficult options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Samper de Calanda?
A: The terrain around Samper de Calanda is diverse, ranging from the fertile &lt;em&gt;Vega del Martín&lt;/em&gt; along the river to routes that follow the Ebro River. You'll find dedicated paths like the "Vía Verde" (a greenway on an old railway line) and "lonely roads" in the broader Matarraña and Bajo Aragón areas, which can include ascents exceeding 1000 meters.

Q: Are there routes suitable for different skill levels in Samper de Calanda?
A: Yes, the region offers routes for various skill levels. There are 4 easy routes, 12 moderate routes, and 3 difficult routes available, ensuring options for beginners to experienced road cyclists.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Samper de Calanda area?
A: Many of the road cycling routes around Samper de Calanda are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e969151762/ruta-del-tambor-y-el-bombo-cycling-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta del Tambor y el Bombo Cycling Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate circular route that explores a culturally significant area.

Q: What are the elevation gains like on road cycling routes around Samper de Calanda?
A: Elevation gains vary significantly across routes. You can find moderate routes with around 280 meters of ascent, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31463408" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Motorland (Alcañiz Circuit) – Fields of the Calanda Desert loop from Puigmoreno&lt;/a&gt;. More challenging routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38151245" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita Virgen Sierra de Arcos – Ariño loop from Urrea de Gaén&lt;/a&gt;, can feature over 2,200 meters of climbing.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I see while road cycling in Samper de Calanda?
A: The routes often pass by significant landmarks. You might encounter the 19th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5025865" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Torica Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt;, or cycle near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6212554" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Martín River and surroundings&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5147620" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albalate del Arzobispo Historic Center&lt;/a&gt;, with its Gothic-Renaissance church and archiepiscopal palace, is also accessible in the wider region.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region offers several scenic spots. The Ermita del Calvario (or Santo Sepulcro), perched on a small hill, provides excellent views over the drylands and the &lt;em&gt;Vega del Martín&lt;/em&gt;. The diverse landscapes, including the Ebro River meanders, also offer picturesque vistas.

Q: Is Samper de Calanda suitable for family-friendly road cycling?
A: While many road cycling routes in the area involve varied terrain and elevation, the "Vía Verde" (greenway) offers a dedicated, flatter path that is generally more suitable for families or those seeking a relaxed ride away from traffic. Always check individual route details for suitability.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Samper de Calanda?
A: The region generally experiences a Mediterranean climate. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, offering milder temperatures and pleasant conditions for cycling. Summers can be hot, especially in July and August, while winters are cooler but often still suitable for cycling on sunny days.

Q: Are there facilities like cafes or accommodation for cyclists in Samper de Calanda?
A: Samper de Calanda and nearby towns offer various amenities. You can find local cafes and restaurants to refuel, and there are accommodation options ranging from hotels to rural houses, catering to visitors exploring the area by bike.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Samper de Calanda?
A: The komoot community frequently praises the diverse landscapes, from river valleys to more challenging ascents, and the opportunity to explore historical sites. Cyclists appreciate the blend of agricultural areas, natural settings, and the quiet, 'lonely roads' that characterize the region.

Q: Can I reach Samper de Calanda by public transport with my bike?
A: While specific public transport options for bikes can vary, it's advisable to check local bus or train services connecting to Samper de Calanda. Policies for transporting bicycles often depend on the operator and the type of service. Planning ahead is recommended.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes for advanced riders?
A: Yes, advanced riders can find challenging long-distance routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19813662" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oliete – Ariño loop from La Puebla de Híjar&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, is a difficult 112 km trail that typically takes over 5 hours to complete, featuring significant elevation changes through the wider region.
tips:
The relief of the municipality is characterized by the so-called Calanda Desert, an arid area with little rainfall, only softened by the presence of the Martín River, which crosses the territory to the west.
The Hermitage of Santa Quiteria is towards the train station and to the left, you come to the hermitage, where water miraculously flows in such an arid land.
The narrow streets of the town of Albalate del Arzobispo, where the town has no flat part. Stairs, stones as walls and sloping streets!

This town at the foot of the Sierra de Arcos and crossed by the Martín River has an urban area that has been declared a Historic-Artistic Site.

The church is Gothic-Renaissance in style except for its tower, which is Mudejar. On the other hand, the archiepiscopal palace castle is representative of the civil Gothic of Bajo Aragonese.

The relief of the municipality is characterized by the presence of the river Martín, a tributary of the Ebro, which creates a characteristic valley near the town. The Martín river valley is a natural area with an important landscape, the steppes being one of the most peculiar environments in the area.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Desierto de Calanda by the Río Martín cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Híjar. You can typically find parking within the town of Híjar, which serves as a convenient starting point for this round trip.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route follows the Río Martín valley, offering a varied relief with undulating terrain. You'll experience a mix of flatter sections and gentle ascents/descents. The broader Desierto de Calanda area features a semi-desert geography, providing unique vistas. Secondary roads in the Teruel province are generally known for low traffic and good asphalt.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Desierto de Calanda by the Río Martín route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass through several interesting points. Highlights include the Samper de Calanda (Hermitage of Santa Quiteria), the charming Alleys of Albalate del Arzobispo, and the Albalate del Arzobispo Historic Center. The starting and ending point, Híjar, is also a notable settlement.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Road cycling routes, especially those on public roads, are generally not recommended for cycling with dogs due to safety concerns with traffic and the sustained pace required. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the semi-desert geography of the Desierto de Calanda, spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for cycling. Summers can be very hot, while winters might be chilly. These shoulder seasons offer milder temperatures and comfortable conditions for enjoying the scenery.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route or the Bajo Martín area?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or to access the general Bajo Martín area. The route primarily uses public roads.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other paths. You'll find it intersects with parts of the Camino del Maestrazgo-Bajo Aragón, Gran travesía del Bajo Martín, Loma del Regadío y ribera del Martín, Barranco de La Hoz - La Silleta, and Sierra de Arcos - Albalate, among others.

faqs.question: What makes the scenery unique on this cycling trip?
faqs.answer: The scenery is quite distinctive due to the Río Martín carving its valley through a semi-desert landscape. You'll experience a striking contrast with steppes and unique geological formations, offering a different visual experience compared to more lush regions.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: The route is rated as 'moderate' difficulty, with approximately 487 meters of elevation gain over 53.5 kilometers. While it features rolling hills and some climbs characteristic of a river valley ride, the roads generally have low traffic and good asphalt. Beginners with a reasonable level of fitness and some experience on longer rides should find it manageable, but it's not entirely flat.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through towns like Albalate del Arzobispo and starts/ends in Híjar. These settlements offer opportunities to find cafes, restaurants, or shops where you can refuel and take a break during your ride.

faqs.question: What is an insider tip for cycling the Desierto de Calanda by the Río Martín route?
faqs.answer: Take advantage of the low traffic and good asphalt on the secondary roads. This allows for a more relaxed and immersive experience, letting you truly appreciate the unique semi-desert landscapes and the quiet beauty of the Río Martín valley. Consider stopping at the abandoned 17th-century Monastery of the Desert of Calanda if your route allows for a slight detour, as it's a significant historical landmark in the area.

waypoints.name: Samper de Calanda (Hermitage of Santa Quiteria)
